name: United States
description: A federal constitutional republic where the President is elected indirectly through the Electoral College, and Congress is elected directly by the people.

eligibility.notes: Must be registered to vote in your state. Registration deadlines vary by state.

keyDates.title: Voter Registration Deadline
keyDates.description: Last day to register to vote for the general election.

keyDates.title: Early Voting Begins
keyDates.description: Many states allow you to vote in person before Election Day.

keyDates.title: Election Day
keyDates.description: The final day to cast your ballot in person.

phases.title: Pre-election Phase
phases.description: Getting ready for the election, including registering and choosing party nominees.

steps.title: Voter Registration

explanations.simple: Before you can vote, you need to sign up. It's like putting your name on the guest list for a party.
explanations.standard: Eligible citizens must register with their state to be allowed to vote. Each state has different deadlines and rules.
explanations.expert: Voter registration is governed at the state level, though federal laws like the NVRA (Motor Voter Act) mandate minimum access standards.

examples: Registering online through your state's election portal.
Registering when you get your driver's license.

glossary.definition: National Voter Registration Act, a law making it easier for Americans to register to vote.

explanations.simple: This is how political parties pick their main player for the big game. People vote for who they want their party to send.
explanations.standard: Before the general election, states hold primary elections or caucuses to determine which candidate will represent each political party.
explanations.expert: Primaries can be open, closed, or semi-closed. Delegates are awarded to candidates based on primary results, which then officially nominate the candidate at the national convention.

glossary.definition: A local meeting where registered members of a political party gather to vote for their preferred candidate.
A state-level election where party members vote to choose a candidate affiliated with their political party.

simple.question: What do you need to do before you can vote?
simple.explanation: You must register (sign up) with your state before you are allowed to vote.

standard.question: What is the main purpose of a primary election?
standard.explanation: Primaries are used to select which candidate will represent a political party in the general election.

expert.question: What is the primary function of the National Voter Registration Act (NVRA) of 1993?
options: To mandate federal voter ID laws
To require states to offer voter registration at DMVs
expert.explanation: The NVRA, or Motor Voter Act, requires states to offer voter registration opportunities at State motor vehicle agencies.

phases.title: Campaign Period
phases.description: Candidates travel, debate, and try to convince people to vote for them.

steps.title: Debates and Rallies

explanations.simple: Candidates answer questions on TV and give big speeches to crowds to show why they should win.
explanations.standard: Candidates hold rallies to energize supporters and participate in televised debates to discuss policy and contrast themselves with opponents.
explanations.expert: The Commission on Presidential Debates historically organized debates, though candidates increasingly negotiate terms directly. Rallies are key for GOTV (Get Out The Vote) efforts.

examples: Presidential debates watched by millions of viewers.

glossary.definition: Get Out The Vote: Campaign strategies to ensure that supporters actually cast their ballots.

simple.question: Why do politicians hold debates on TV?
options: To answer questions and show why they should win
simple.explanation: Debates let voters hear candidates answer important questions side-by-side.

standard.question: What does GOTV stand for?
standard.explanation: GOTV stands for 'Get Out The Vote', which focuses on ensuring supporters actually go and vote.

expert.question: Historically, which entity has organized the general election presidential debates?
options: The League of Women Voters exclusively
expert.explanation: Since 1988, the bipartisan Commission on Presidential Debates (CPD) has sponsored the general election debates.

phases.title: Voting Day
phases.description: The time when citizens cast their ballots.

explanations.simple: You go to a voting place or mail your ballot to make your choice secret.
explanations.standard: Voters go to their assigned polling place to cast a secret ballot. Many states also allow mail-in or early voting.
explanations.expert: Elections use various voting methods including hand-marked paper ballots and Direct Recording Electronic (DRE) machines, ensuring the secret ballot principle.

glossary.definition: A voting method in which a voter's choices in an election are anonymous.

simple.question: Is your vote a secret?
options: Only your family can see it
simple.explanation: The US uses a 'secret ballot', meaning no one knows who you voted for.

standard.question: Which of these is NOT a common way to vote in the US?
standard.explanation: Voting via text message is not allowed due to security and verification concerns.

expert.question: What is a DRE voting machine?
expert.explanation: DRE (Direct Recording Electronic) machines record votes directly into the computer's memory.

phases.title: Post-election Phase
phases.description: Counting votes and making it official.

explanations.simple: The US President isn't picked by who gets the most votes overall, but by winning states. Each state has a certain number of points.
explanations.standard: The President is elected by the Electoral College, not the popular vote. States have electors based on their population, and 270 electoral votes are needed to win.
explanations.expert: Most states use a winner-take-all system for their electoral votes. If no candidate reaches 270, the House of Representatives chooses the President in a contingent election.

glossary.definition: A body of electors established by the US Constitution, which forms every four years for the sole purpose of electing the president and vice president.
The total number or percentage of votes cast for a candidate by voters in the 50 states and Washington, D.C.

simple.question: Does the person with the most total votes across the whole country always become President?
options: No, they must win the Electoral College points
Only if they get 100% of votes
simple.explanation: A candidate can get more total votes but still lose if they don't win enough state 'points' in the Electoral College.

standard.question: How many Electoral College votes are needed to win the US Presidency?
standard.explanation: A candidate needs a majority of the 538 electoral votes to win, which is 270.

expert.question: What happens if no presidential candidate secures a majority (270 votes) in the Electoral College?
options: A national runoff election is held
The current President stays in power
The House of Representatives chooses the President
expert.explanation: In a contingent election, the House of Representatives elects the President, with each state delegation having one vote.

faqs.question: What is gerrymandering?
faqs.answer: Gerrymandering is the practice of drawing the boundaries of electoral districts in a way that gives one political party an unfair advantage over its rivals.

faqs.question: What if there's a tie in the Electoral College?
faqs.answer: If there is a tie (e.g., 269-269), the election goes to the House of Representatives, where each state delegation gets one vote.
